priorities: Local Swiss sourcing and brand authenticity
Data‑driven operational efficiency and cost‑of‑capital focus
Art collection as an alternative investment
redLines: No holdings in any company linked to sweatshops, illegal exploitation or wage‑theft
Immediate divestment if a portfolio company is hit by a labour or governance scandal, even before mainstream press coverage
Zero tolerance for any supply‑chain contamination that could be associated with the client’s name
Reject static ESG scores; require live monitoring of media/judicial alerts on labour practices
Avoid firms that do not meet EU CSDDD supply‑chain due‑diligence standards
preferredSectors: Swiss consumer retail and premium domestic brands
European consumer discretionary with strong ESG governance
Logistics and automation providers that publish transparent labour data
Blue‑chip consumer and apparel companies with verified clean supply chains
Emerging Swiss art market (as a niche alternative asset)
avoidedSectors: Fast‑fashion and low‑cost apparel manufacturing with opaque supply chains
Companies operating in jurisdictions with weak labour enforcement
Firms with historic ESG violations or ongoing labour‑rights scandals
Energy‑intensive businesses lacking clear carbon‑management and labour standards
esgFocus: Labour rights and supply‑chain due diligence
Energy‑cost sensitivity for logistics and industrial holdings

investmentBehavior.timeHorizon: Long‑term (5‑10+ years) aligned with brand building and legacy objectives
investmentBehavior.liquidity: Maintains short‑term cash for tax and art purchases; prefers high‑quality short‑term cantonal instruments while keeping a balanced, moderately liquid portfolio

lifeEvents.description: Announced the opening of the 38th Ammann store in Lausanne, emphasizing local sourcing and transparent supply chains.
lifeEvents.portfolioImpact: Reinforces focus on Swiss domestic retail exposure and reinforces clean‑brand positioning.

lifeEvents.description: Acquired a work by a young Zurich‑based sculptor at the Kunsthaus auction, linking art support to Swiss cultural identity.
lifeEvents.portfolioImpact: Introduces art as a niche alternative‑investment class; may allocate discretionary capital to emerging Swiss artists.

lifeEvents.description: Delivered keynote on "Supply Chain Integrity as a Competitive Moat" at the Zurich Governance Forum.
lifeEvents.portfolioImpact: Publicly re‑affirms the zero‑tolerance stance; likely triggers tighter screening of existing holdings.

lifeEvents.description: Formal handover of the global balanced portfolio due to senior RM Thomas Keller’s retirement.
lifeEvents.portfolioImpact: Transition period; client stresses continuity of strict governance screening with new RM.

lifeEvents.description: Introductory call with new RM Sarah Meier, reiterating the clean‑governance fortress requirement.
lifeEvents.portfolioImpact: Confirms ongoing enforcement of the zero‑tolerance mandate across all asset classes.

interests: Corporate governance and ESG supply‑chain issues
quirks: Insists on real‑time scandal monitoring over static ESG ratings
Will liquidate a position before any negative press can link his name to it
Highly analytical, constantly cross‑referencing retail data with portfolio metrics
rapportTriggers: Discussion of supply‑chain transparency and EU CSDDD
Swiss provenance stories and local brand authenticity
Zurich governance forums and corporate ethics debates
Emerging Swiss artists and recent auction pieces
avoidTopics: Generic ESG scores without operational evidence
Political debates unrelated to business or ESG

keyQuotes.quote: My national brand equity is built on clean labor and local integrity. If my name is linked via my portfolio to an international corporate parent caught using sweatshops, illegal exploitation, or wage theft, it creates immediate hypocrisy risk for my own stores.
If any international consumer or retail brand we hold gets hit with severe labor exploitation allegations, dump the entire position before the local Swiss press can link my name to it.

Standard glossy ESG reporting and backward‑looking sustainability scores are useless to me. They are purely retrospective and completely miss live operational risk or unfolding scandals. I don't care if a company has an 'AA' ESG rating on paper—if there is live media or judicial smoke surrounding their labor practices, the position must be flagged immediately before it hits mainstream national press.
Supply chain integrity is a competitive moat; clean operations are not an ethical luxury — they are the most durable form of brand equity.
The EU Supply Chain Due Diligence Act is not a burden for responsible companies — it is a competitive advantage.
